review_notes: Independent re-verification. WEBFETCH STATUS: WebFetch returned HTTP 403 on EVERY URL attempted this session (Wikipedia, lassoism, ScreenRant, ReadySteadyCut, and the tvwriting.co.uk production-script PDF), exactly as warned. Following the Season-1 norm, no verdict rests on a single fetched page: each fact is corroborated across MULTIPLE INDEPENDENT WebSearch result summaries from DIFFERENT domains (Wikipedia, TV Tropes, Slate, ScreenRant, PureWow, SlashFilm, TV Insider, Cheat Sheet, NPR, The Spool, ReadySteadyCut, GoldDerby, Tunefind/WhatSong, lassoism transcript text surfaced in results, plus a contemporaneous tweet for the Bantr handles). Because no primary page opened, GROUNDING IS CAPPED AT 4 for every fact. CREDITS confirmed: written by Bill Wrubel, directed by Erica Dunton, released Aug 20 2021, S2E5 / 15th episode overall (Wikipedia + Fandom + the production-script header text + IMDb full-credits listing all agree); the 'Jane Becker' writer attribution from an initial briefing-style search is WRONG and was rejected. FLAG RESOLUTIONS: (1) RILKE -- treated exactly like the show's Whitman misattribution. The in-show moment (Rebecca's Bantr suitor quotes a line credited to Rilke, she swoons) is real; the line 'our deepest fears are like dragons guarding our deepest treasure' is a widely-circulated PARAPHRASE attributed to Rilke, NOT a located verbatim Rilke sentence -- the genuine Letters to a Young Poet passage is the 'dragons are really princesses' line (confirmed via Goodreads/AZQuotes vs. the paraphrase's own quote-site pages). groundingStatus=attributed; the card does NOT assert it as true verbatim Rilke. (2) DANTE -- NOT asserted. The show's text frames the 'dark forest' purely as fairy-tale imagery ('Fairy tales do not start, nor do they end in the dark forest'); the Dante/selva-oscura link appears only as unconfirmed fan/recapper inference, so the card presents it as a fairy-tale 'middle of the story' motif and explicitly notes Dante is not confirmed. (3) 'SHE'S A RAINBOW' PLACEMENT -- RESOLVED in favor of S2E5. Multiple independent sources place the Rolling Stones' 'She's a Rainbow' at this episode's close (Roy's run to the stadium / Higgins's airport-style reunion), and it is the apparent title source; the S1E5 'Tan Lines' tag in docs/factsheet.md sec.6 is marked [INFERRED] and appears to be a source error -- independent listings give 'Tan Lines' its own featured song (Mumford & Sons 'Forever'), not 'She's a Rainbow'. Flagged for a human to correct the factsheet; the S2E5 card ships only the verified S2E5 placement. (4) EMMY SUBMISSION -- DELIBERATELY WITHHELD from the shipped card per the brief. GoldDerby does report 'Rainbow' as Brett Goldstein's 2022 (Season 2) Emmy submission, but the brief forbids asserting it and I keep the production card to firmly-sourced credits/air-date only; noted here for the record, not shipped. (5) ROM-COM READS -- the Sleepless in Seattle exit, You've Got Mail Bantr handles, Love Actually reunion, and Nate beat are recapper interpretations; I verified each independently and scaled claims to what corroborates. The You've Got Mail handles 'Bossgirl' (Rebecca) and 'LDN152' (suitor) -- vs. 'Shopgirl'/'NY152' -- are confirmed by ScreenRant's dedicated explainer, PureWow, and a contemporaneous tweet, so they are stated; the Love Actually 'matching camera filter' and the Nate 'Pretty Woman' framing are recapper observations and are softened or dropped. NATE: the restaurant ('A Taste of Athens', Tooting), hostess Jade, the Rebecca/Keeley 'make yourself big' power-pose coaching, and Nate spitting at his reflection are all firmly corroborated; the 'Pretty Woman' label specifically is a thin recapper read I could not independently confirm, so that fact is partially-confirmed and its card is reframed on the confidence/assertiveness arc rather than asserting Pretty Woman.

facts.verification_notes: Independently confirmed across Wikipedia, PopMatters, PureWow, TV Insider, Looper and the lassoism transcript text that surfaced in results: with the team struggling, Ted declares he believes in 'communism' then clarifies 'rom-communism' -- a worldview from romantic comedies that everything works out in the end -- and he and the squad name rom-com stars (Tom Hanks, Meg Ryan, Julia Roberts, Hugh Grant, then Drew Barrymore, McConaughey, etc.). Clearly placed in S2E5 'Rainbow'. WebFetch 403 on all pages, so grounding capped at 4.

card.title: Ted's 'rom-communism' speech
card.moment: With the team slumping, Ted announces he believes in 'communism' -- then, after a beat, 'rom-communism' -- his made-up word for a worldview lifted from romantic comedies: if everyone pulls together, it all works out in the end. He and the players reel off the genre's biggest stars.
card.idea: A comic coinage that's really the show's whole creed in costume: hope as a deliberate choice. Like a rom-com, the promise isn't that nothing goes wrong, it's that you trust the happy ending and keep showing up for each other.
card.why: This is the spine of the episode's romantic-comedy homage. Notice it's precursive faith -- belief offered before the evidence, dressed as a movie-genre joke so it lands light instead of preachy.
card.source: Episode recaps and essays on the 'rom-communism' speech (PopMatters, PureWow, TV Insider, Looper).
card.groundingNotes: Speech and star name-drops confirmed across multiple independent recaps; placement in S2E5 firm. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), so grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed via MagicalQuote, getyarn (clip tagged S02E05 'Rainbow'), Slate, NPR and the lassoism transcript text in results: extending the rom-communism speech, Ted reframes the team's rough patch as their 'dark forest', noting fairy tales never begin or end in the dark forest -- it shows up in the middle -- so it will all work out. The show frames this strictly as fairy-tale imagery. A Dante/'selva oscura' reading appears only as unconfirmed recapper inference; the show never invokes Dante, so the card presents a fairy-tale motif and does NOT assert Dante. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: The middle of every story
card.moment: Still rallying the team, Ted calls the hard stretch they're in their 'dark forest' -- and reminds them that in fairy tales the dark forest never comes at the beginning or the end, only the middle. So they'll come out the other side, even if not the way they expect.
card.idea: Suffering reframed as structure, not verdict: the low point is a necessary middle act, not the ending. It's narrative-shaped optimism -- a way to hold on through the worst stretch by trusting that a story is still being told.
card.why: A lovely piece of hope you can actually use: when you're in the dark forest, that's evidence you're in the middle, not the end. (The image is fairy-tale lore -- some viewers hear an echo of Dante's 'dark wood', but the show only ever names fairy tales, so enjoy the resonance without treating Dante as intended.)
card.source: Episode recaps and quote pages for Ted's 'dark forest' speech (MagicalQuote, Slate, NPR; clip via getyarn).
card.groundingNotes: Fairy-tale 'middle of the story' speech confirmed across recaps and the clip; Dante allusion is unconfirmed fan inference and deliberately not asserted. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed across TV Insider, ScreenRant, Cheat Sheet, PureWow and Slate: to recruit Roy as a coach, Ted strings together rewritten famous rom-com lines -- the 'rest of your life' beat (When Harry Met Sally), 'you complete...' (Jerry Maguire), the 'just a ___ standing in front of a ___' construction (Notting Hill), and 'as you wish' (The Princess Bride). Films firmly and independently sourced; card paraphrases and reproduces no dialogue. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: Ted woos Roy with the movies
card.moment: Trying to talk Roy into coaching, Ted courts him like a leading man proposing -- a medley of rewritten rom-com declarations: the 'rest of your life' speech, a 'you complete...' line, the 'I'm just a ___ standing in front of a ___' construction, and finally 'as you wish.'
card.idea: It's 'rom-communism' acted out: Ted literally stages Roy's recruitment as a romantic-comedy proposal, borrowing the genre's biggest love-confession beats to say 'I believe in you, join us.'
card.why: A rewatch reward -- see how many films you can place. The joke that Ted means every word is the heart of it: the grand romantic gesture, redirected at a friendship and a job.
card.source: Reference breakdowns identifying the When Harry Met Sally / Jerry Maguire / Notting Hill / Princess Bride lines (TV Insider, ScreenRant, Cheat Sheet, PureWow).
card.groundingNotes: The four films Ted quotes confirmed across multiple independent breakdowns. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed via PureWow, ScreenRant, Cheat Sheet and ReadySteadyCut recaps: after twice declining, Roy leaves his pundit desk mid-broadcast, races to the stadium, and accepts by riffing on Jerry Maguire's 'you had me at hello' as 'You had me at coach' -- paying off Ted's earlier 'you complete...' line with the genre's roles playfully reversed. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: Roy's grand gesture
card.moment: Having turned Ted down twice, Roy is jolted by the crowd's love for the club, bolts from his TV panel mid-show, and dashes to the stadium -- accepting the coaching job with a riff on Jerry Maguire: 'You had me at coach.'
card.idea: The episode's romantic climax, with the genre's roles swapped: the gruff, guarded man makes the breathless run and the big confession. Roy stops performing detachment and admits how much he still loves the game.
card.why: Watch it pay off Ted's earlier 'you complete our team' pitch -- the recruitment really was a courtship, and this is the yes. The toughest character gets the softest, most movie-perfect moment.
card.source: Recaps of Roy leaving the studio and accepting the job (PureWow, ScreenRant, Cheat Sheet).
card.groundingNotes: Roy's studio exit and 'you had me at coach' acceptance confirmed across multiple recaps. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed via SlashFilm, ScreenRant and PureWow: as Roy quits the broadcast to chase the job, his abrupt exit line to co-host Jeff Stelling is widely read as a Sleepless in Seattle nod to Meg Ryan's climactic break with Walter. (Recappers also note the team therapist Dr. Fieldstone shares a name with Sleepless's Dr. Marcia Fieldstone.) This is a corroborated recapper interpretation, not a creator confirmation. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: Roy's studio exit
card.moment: Walking out on the broadcast to go accept the job, Roy abruptly tells his fellow pundit he has to leave -- a beat recappers flag as an echo of Meg Ryan's climactic exit in Sleepless in Seattle.
card.idea: One more rom-com stitch in an episode built from them: the moment of leaving the safe, wrong thing for the risky, right one, lifted from a classic of the genre.
card.why: Easy to miss on a first watch. A small bonus: the team's therapist, Dr. Fieldstone, shares a surname with Sleepless's radio shrink, Dr. Marcia Fieldstone -- the homages run deep this week.
card.source: Rom-com reference guides identifying the Sleepless in Seattle nod (SlashFilm, ScreenRant, PureWow).
card.groundingNotes: Widely-noted recapper reading across multiple outlets (and the Dr. Fieldstone name parallel); not creator-confirmed. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: TWO-PART finding, treated like the show's Whitman misattribution. IN-SHOW FACT CONFIRMED: on the anonymous Bantr app, Rebecca's mystery suitor quotes a line credited to Rilke -- roughly that our deepest fears are dragons guarding our deepest treasure -- and she's smitten (Wikipedia, ScreenRant, lassoism transcript text in results). ATTRIBUTION FLAG CONFIRMED: that exact line is a widely-circulated PARAPHRASE attributed to Rilke, NOT a located verbatim quote; the genuine Letters to a Young Poet passage is the 'dragons are really princesses waiting to see us beautiful and brave' line (Goodreads/AZQuotes/Quotefancy carry the paraphrase under Rilke's name, while Rilke's authentic text is the princess passage). So groundingStatus=attributed; the card states it's a popular line attributed to Rilke and does NOT assert it as verbatim Rilke. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: Rebecca's mystery poet
card.moment: Flirting on the anonymous Bantr app, Rebecca's unseen match quotes a line credited to the poet Rainer Maria Rilke -- that our deepest fears are like dragons guarding our deepest treasure -- and she melts.
card.idea: Here's the catch a literature lover will love: that exact line is one of the internet's most popular 'Rilke' quotes, but it's a circulating paraphrase attributed to him, not a sentence anyone has located in his work. Rilke's genuine, nearby passage (from Letters to a Young Poet) is the gentler one about dragons in our lives perhaps being princesses, waiting to see us act with beauty and courage.
card.why: A perfect twin to the show's own 'be curious, not judgmental' Whitman mix-up: a beautiful idea, sincerely meant, hung on an attribution that won't quite hold. Enjoy the sentiment -- and know it's 'attributed to Rilke,' not verified as his words.
card.source: Quote-site listings carry the line under Rilke's name (Goodreads, AZQuotes) while his authentic Letters to a Young Poet 'dragons/princesses' passage differs; in-show moment per episode reference guides (ScreenRant).
card.groundingNotes: In-show 'Rilke' quote confirmed; the line itself is a popular paraphrase attributed to Rilke, not a located verbatim quote (his real passage is the 'dragons as princesses' one). No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed and handle spellings checked: on Bantr, Rebecca's handle is 'Bossgirl' and her suitor's is 'LDN152' -- mirroring 'Shopgirl' and 'NY152' from You've Got Mail (Joe Fox's NY number becomes a London number). Corroborated by ScreenRant's dedicated LDN152 explainer, PureWow, SlashFilm, and a contemporaneous 2021 tweet noting the parallel. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: The screen names tell the story
card.moment: On Bantr, Rebecca messages under the handle 'Bossgirl' with a suitor called 'LDN152' -- screen names that mirror 'Shopgirl' and 'NY152' from You've Got Mail, where two strangers fall for each other anonymously online.
card.idea: The whole mystery-suitor subplot is quietly built on one rom-com: 'NY152' (New York) becomes 'LDN152' (London), 'Shopgirl' becomes 'Bossgirl.' A precise little Easter egg that tells you what kind of love story this is before it plays out.
card.why: Look closely at the handles on screen -- the parallel is exact. It's a wink that rewards anyone who knows the film, and it sets up a romance about two people who connect as words before they connect as people.
card.source: ScreenRant's LDN152 explainer plus SlashFilm and PureWow reference guides (and contemporaneous fan notes) on the You've Got Mail handle parallel.
card.groundingNotes: Handles 'Bossgirl'/'LDN152' vs. 'Shopgirl'/'NY152' confirmed across a dedicated explainer, reference guides, and contemporaneous fan notes. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed via ScreenRant, Yahoo/PureWow, Cheat Sheet and Slate: the episode uses When Harry Met Sally's talking-to-camera elderly-couple device, then lands a joke -- one couple's love story is secretly the plot of Titanic (he sketched her, the ship sank), and they claim James Cameron stole it and they're in litigation. A double-decker reference (one film's format smuggling in another's story). WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: The couple who 'are' Titanic
card.moment: Borrowing When Harry Met Sally's device of old couples telling the camera how they met, the episode lands a gag: one pair's romance turns out, beat for beat, to be the plot of Titanic -- and they insist James Cameron stole their story and they're now suing him.
card.idea: A reference folded inside a reference: the format of one beloved romance (those direct-to-camera interviews) is used to smuggle in a completely different blockbuster love story, then capped with an absurd litigation punchline.
card.why: A real 'I never caught that' beat. If you know both films it pays off twice -- once for the When Harry Met Sally framing, once when you realize whose story they're actually telling.
card.source: Reference breakdowns describing the elderly-couple device and the Titanic reveal (ScreenRant, PureWow, Cheat Sheet, Slate).
card.groundingNotes: When Harry Met Sally fourth-wall device plus the Titanic/Cameron gag confirmed across multiple independent recaps. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed via SlashFilm, PureWow and TV Tropes: a montage of fans and partners reuniting before the match -- including Higgins waiting for his wife, scored to 'She's a Rainbow' -- evokes Love Actually's arrivals-gate reunions. The 'matching camera filter' detail is a recapper observation, not a cinematographer statement, and is framed as such. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: Reunions before the match
card.moment: A montage of fans and loved ones reuniting before the game -- Higgins among them, waiting for his wife -- echoes the arrivals-gate reunions that open Love Actually, the Rolling Stones playing over it.
card.idea: The episode quietly extends its 'everyone gets their happy reunion' mood to the background characters, borrowing Love Actually's warmest image: ordinary people, overjoyed to see the person they love.
card.why: An easy one to glide past. It folds the side characters into the rom-com theme and sets the tender tone the episode pays off. (Recappers note the footage even apes the film's washed, glowy airport look.)
card.source: Reference guides identifying the Love Actually reunion montage and Higgins waiting (SlashFilm, PureWow; TV Tropes recap).
card.groundingNotes: Reunion montage / Higgins-waiting as a Love Actually homage confirmed across recaps; the 'matching filter' detail is a recapper observation, not creator-confirmed. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: The in-episode plot is firmly confirmed (Wikipedia, The Spool, TV Tropes, lassoism transcript text): Nate wants the window table at his parents' favorite Greek restaurant ('A Taste of Athens' in Tooting) for their anniversary, is brushed off by hostess Jade, and Rebecca and Keeley coach him on confidence -- Rebecca demonstrates her 'make yourself big' power-pose (an Amy-Cuddy-style stance ending in a hiss); Nate later psychs himself up by spitting at his own reflection and gets the table. The specific 'Pretty Woman' framing, however, is a thin recapper read I could NOT independently corroborate beyond a single source, so the verdict is partially-confirmed and the card is reframed on the (well-sourced) confidence/assertiveness arc rather than asserting Pretty Woman. Spoiler_safety lowered because the beat foreshadows Nate's season-long turn. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.
facts.curator_note: Reframed away from 'Pretty Woman': that label is an under-sourced recapper read (verdict partially-confirmed). The card ships the firmly-corroborated material -- the restaurant/table, hostess Jade, Rebecca & Keeley's 'make yourself big' power-pose coaching, and Nate spitting at his reflection. The beat foreshadows Nate's later turn (note the dark edge), so spoiler-scope leans season.

card.title: Nate learns to ask
card.moment: Desperate to land the window table at his parents' favorite Greek place for their anniversary, an insecure Nate is brushed off by the hostess. Rebecca and Keeley coach him -- Rebecca shows off her pre-big-moment ritual of 'making herself big' to summon her power -- and Nate, after a strange little pep-talk to his own reflection, comes back and confidently claims the table.
card.idea: The flip side of the episode's confidence theme. Where the rom-coms promise that good things arrive if you just believe, Nate learns you sometimes have to ask -- to take up space and state what you want out loud.
card.why: Tender and a little uneasy at once: it's a real growth moment, but watch how he psychs himself up. The same hunger that helps him here is the seed of where his story darkens later.
card.source: Episode recaps and the Ted Lasso wiki for the 'A Taste of Athens' anniversary-table plot and the Rebecca/Keeley confidence coaching (The Spool, TV Tropes, Fandom).
card.groundingNotes: Restaurant/table plot, hostess Jade, and the 'make yourself big' power-pose coaching firmly corroborated; the 'Pretty Woman' framing is an under-sourced recapper read and is not asserted. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Confirmed via ReadySteadyCut, TV Tropes, The Spool and Wikipedia: before formally becoming a coach, Roy is tapped to fix a spark-less Isaac. He takes Isaac (and Ted) to a concrete pitch under an underpass to watch a pickup game and reminds him football is a kid's game meant to be fun; Isaac loosens up and dominates, scored to Blur's 'Song 2'. Seeds Roy's coaching vocation and the show's 'play for joy' theme. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.

card.title: Roy takes Isaac back
card.moment: Asked to fix a slumping team captain, Roy brings Isaac (and Ted) to a rough pitch under an underpass to watch a pickup game -- reminding him football is a kid's game you played because it was fun. Isaac's joy, and his form, come roaring back.
card.idea: A callback to the show's recurring idea that joy, not pressure, is where good play lives. Roy diagnoses the problem as fear and overthinking, and the cure is simply remembering why you started.
card.why: This is where Roy quietly discovers he can coach -- the seed of his whole next chapter, paid off later in the very same episode. Watch a man who's all gruffness turn out to be a natural teacher.
card.source: Recaps of Roy's underpass intervention with Isaac and his revival, scored to Blur's 'Song 2' (ReadySteadyCut, TV Tropes, The Spool).
card.groundingNotes: Underpass intervention, the 'football should be fun' message, and Isaac's turnaround confirmed across multiple recaps. No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.

facts.verification_notes: Credits confirmed across Wikipedia, the Ted Lasso Fandom episode page, the IMDb full-credits listing, and the tvwriting.co.uk production-script header text surfaced in results: 'Rainbow' is S2E5 (15th episode overall), written by Bill Wrubel, directed by Erica Dunton, released Aug 20 2021. The 'Jane Becker' writer credit from an initial briefing-style search is WRONG and rejected. NOTE: GoldDerby reports 'Rainbow' as Brett Goldstein's 2022 Emmy submission, but per the brief this is NOT asserted in the card. The fact is verified but its interest_score (58) falls below the 60 threshold, so it does not pass the include gate.

Confirmed for S2E5 across Wikipedia, ReadySteadyCut, TV Tropes and the Tunefind/WhatSong S2E5 listings: the Rolling Stones' 'She's a Rainbow' plays at the episode's close (over Roy's run to the stadium and Higgins's reunion) and is the apparent title source; Blur's 'Song 2' scores the earlier Isaac montage. FACTSHEET TENSION RESOLVED toward S2E5: independent listings give S1E5 'Tan Lines' a different featured song (Mumford & Sons 'Forever'), so docs/factsheet.md sec.6's [INFERRED] S1E5 tag for 'She's a Rainbow' appears to be a source error -- flagged for human correction. WebFetch 403, grounding capped at 4.
facts.curator_note: Resolves the factsheet tension: 'She's a Rainbow' is verified at the close of S2E5 'Rainbow' (likely the title source). docs/factsheet.md sec.6 tags it [INFERRED] to S1E5 'Tan Lines', but independent listings give 'Tan Lines' a different featured song -- the S1E5 tag looks like a source error and should be corrected by a human.

card.title: The title and the closer
card.moment: The episode's name lands on two things at once -- its rom-com 'happy ending after the storm' mood, and its music: the Rolling Stones' 'She's a Rainbow' plays it out, over the reunions and Roy's dash to the stadium. (Earlier, Blur's 'Song 2' powered Isaac's on-pitch revival.)
card.idea: A title doing double duty: the rainbow as the rom-communism promise that brightness follows the storm, sealed by a song that becomes 'the song' of Higgins and his wife.
card.why: Stay through the end and hear how the music names the theme. After an episode about belief paying off, the closing song is the show letting the sun out.
card.source: S2E5 soundtrack listings (Tunefind, WhatSong) and recaps placing 'She's a Rainbow' at the close and Blur's 'Song 2' on the Isaac montage (ReadySteadyCut, TV Tropes).
card.groundingNotes: 'She's a Rainbow' verified at the close of S2E5 across soundtrack listings and recaps; a factsheet tag placing it in S1E5 appears to be a source error (S1E5's featured song differs). No primary page opened (WebFetch 403), grounding capped at 4.
